examples.explanation:
Initialize: matchU = [-1,-1,-1], matchV = [-1,-1,-1], max_matching = 0

Vertex U0: DFS to find augmenting path
  Try V3: unmatched, match U0-V3
  matchU[0]=3, matchV[3]=0, max_matching=1

Vertex U1: DFS to find augmenting path
  Try V3: matched to U0, try to find alternate path from U0
    U0 can try V4: unmatched, match U0-V4, unmatch U0-V3
    Backtrack: matchU[0]=4, matchV[4]=0, matchV[3]=-1
  Now V3 is free, match U1-V3
  matchU[1]=3, matchV[3]=1, max_matching=2

Vertex U2: DFS to find augmenting path
  Try V4: matched to U0, try to find alternate path from U0
    U0 can try V3: matched to U1, try to find alternate path from U1
      U1 can try V5: unmatched, match U1-V5, unmatch U1-V3
      Backtrack: matchU[1]=5, matchV[5]=1, matchV[3]=-1
    Now V3 is free, match U0-V3, unmatch U0-V4
    Backtrack: matchU[0]=3, matchV[3]=0, matchV[4]=-1
  Now V4 is free, match U2-V4
  matchU[2]=4, matchV[4]=2, max_matching=3

All vertices matched, return 3

Initialize: matchU = [-1,-1], matchV = [-1,-1], max_matching = 0

Vertex U0: DFS to find augmenting path
  Try V2: unmatched, match U0-V2
  matchU[0]=2, matchV[2]=0, max_matching=1

Vertex U1: DFS to find augmenting path
  Try V2: matched to U0, try to find alternate path from U0
    U0 can try V3: unmatched, match U0-V3, unmatch U0-V2
    Backtrack: matchU[0]=3, matchV[3]=0, matchV[2]=-1
  Now V2 is free, match U1-V2
  matchU[1]=2, matchV[2]=1, max_matching=2

All vertices matched, return 2

approaches.name: Brute Force DFS O(V*E)
approaches.summary: Iterate through all vertices in one partition and try to match each by exploring all possible augmenting paths via DFS. This is a naive method that repeatedly searches for any augmenting path without optimization.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force DFS Bipartite Matching
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of maximum bipartite matching using naive DFS approach
ladders.explanation: This is the complete solution for finding maximum bipartite matching using brute force DFS. The algorithm works by treating the bipartite graph as having two partitions (U and V). For each unmatched vertex in partition U, we perform DFS to find an augmenting path - a path that starts and ends at unmatched vertices, alternating between unmatched and matched edges. When found, we flip the matching status along the path (augmenting), increasing the matching size by 1. The intuition is that each augmenting path allows us to increase the matching by rearranging existing matches. We iterate through all vertices in U, attempting to match each one via DFS exploration of all possible paths. The algorithm terminates when no more augmenting paths can be found, which by Berge's lemma means we have a maximum matching. Time complexity is O(V*E) because in worst case, each DFS explores all edges for each vertex. Space complexity is O(V+E) for adjacency list and recursion stack. This connects to the key subroutine (L1) as the main driver that repeatedly calls the DFS matching function. Common mistakes include not resetting visited arrays between DFS calls, forgetting to handle the alternating path property correctly, and infinite recursion in cyclic graphs (though bipartite graphs are acyclic).

examples.trace:
Initialize: matchU = [-1,-1,-1], matchV = [-1,-1,-1], max_matching = 0

Vertex U0: DFS to find augmenting path
  Try V3: unmatched, match U0-V3
  matchU[0]=3, matchV[3]=0, max_matching=1

Vertex U1: DFS to find augmenting path
  Try V3: matched to U0, try to find alternate path from U0
    U0 can try V4: unmatched, match U0-V4, unmatch U0-V3
    Backtrack: matchU[0]=4, matchV[4]=0, matchV[3]=-1
  Now V3 is free, match U1-V3
  matchU[1]=3, matchV[3]=1, max_matching=2

Vertex U2: DFS to find augmenting path
  Try V4: matched to U0, try to find alternate path from U0
    U0 can try V3: matched to U1, try to find alternate path from U1
      U1 can try V5: unmatched, match U1-V5, unmatch U1-V3
      Backtrack: matchU[1]=5, matchV[5]=1, matchV[3]=-1
    Now V3 is free, match U0-V3, unmatch U0-V4
    Backtrack: matchU[0]=3, matchV[3]=0, matchV[4]=-1
  Now V4 is free, match U2-V4
  matchU[2]=4, matchV[4]=2, max_matching=3

All vertices matched, return 3

Initialize: matchU = [-1,-1], matchV = [-1,-1], max_matching = 0

Vertex U0: DFS to find augmenting path
  Try V2: unmatched, match U0-V2
  matchU[0]=2, matchV[2]=0, max_matching=1

Vertex U1: DFS to find augmenting path
  Try V2: matched to U0, try to find alternate path from U0
    U0 can try V3: unmatched, match U0-V3, unmatch U0-V2
    Backtrack: matchU[0]=3, matchV[3]=0, matchV[2]=-1
  Now V2 is free, match U1-V2
  matchU[1]=2, matchV[2]=1, max_matching=2

All vertices matched, return 2

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS Augmenting Path Search
ladders.desc: DFS function that finds an augmenting path for a specific vertex
ladders.explanation: This is the core DFS function that attempts to find an augmenting path starting from a given unmatched vertex u in partition U. The function explores all neighbors of u in partition V. For each neighbor v, if v is unmatched, we've found an augmenting path of length 1 (u-v). If v is already matched to some u', we recursively try to find an augmenting path starting from u'. This creates the alternating path property: unmatched edge, matched edge, unmatched edge, etc. The intuition is that we're looking for a path where we can 'rearrange' existing matches to free up a vertex for matching. The DFS returns True if an augmenting path is found, and updates the matching arrays accordingly. Time complexity for one DFS call is O(E) in worst case as it explores all edges. Space complexity is O(V) for recursion depth. This connects to the full problem (L0) as the function called for each unmatched vertex. Common mistakes include not marking vertices as visited during the DFS (leading to infinite loops), not properly backtracking when a path doesn't work out, and incorrect handling of the alternating path logic.

examples.input: Current matching: U0-V3, U1 unmatched. Try to match U1. Graph: U1 connects to V3,V5
examples.trace:
Start DFS from U1
  Try neighbor V3: V3 is matched to U0
    Recursively DFS from U0
      U0 has neighbors V3,V4
      Try V3: already visited in this path (would create cycle)
      Try V4: unmatched
        Found augmenting path: U1-V3-U0-V4
        Update: match U1-V3, match U0-V4, unmatch U0-V3
        Return True
  Return True

examples.input: Current matching: U0-V2, U1-V3. Try to match U2. Graph: U2 connects to V2 only
examples.trace:
Start DFS from U2
  Try neighbor V2: V2 is matched to U0
    Recursively DFS from U0
      U0 has neighbors V2,V3
      Try V2: already visited
      Try V3: matched to U1
        Recursively DFS from U1
          U1 has neighbors V3 only
          Try V3: already visited
          No more neighbors, return False
      All neighbors tried, return False
    Return False
  Return False

ladders.title: Core Logic — Alternating Path Augmentation
ladders.desc: The concept of finding and augmenting alternating paths to increase matching
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the core insight of bipartite matching algorithms: the augmenting path theorem. An augmenting path is a path that starts at an unmatched vertex in U, ends at an unmatched vertex in V, and alternates between unmatched and matched edges. When we find such a path, we can 'augment' the matching by flipping the status of all edges along the path - unmatched edges become matched, and matched edges become unmatched. This increases the total matching size by exactly 1. The intuition is that we're rearranging existing matches to make room for one more match. This works because bipartite graphs have the property that augmenting paths don't create conflicts. The algorithm repeatedly finds augmenting paths until none exist, at which point we have a maximum matching (Berge's lemma). This is the central technique that makes the brute force DFS approach work. Time complexity analysis shows each augmentation takes O(E) time via DFS. This connects to the key subroutine (L1) as the theoretical foundation for why DFS search works. Common mistakes include not understanding why alternating paths are necessary, trying to augment non-alternating paths, or not realizing that augmentation always increases matching size by exactly 1.

examples.input: Matching: U0-V1. Find augmenting path from U2 through U0
examples.output: Augment: U2-V0, U0-V1 becomes U0-V0, U2-V1
examples.trace:
Current matching edges: U0-V1
Unmatched vertices: U2, V0
Find augmenting path: U2 → V1 → U0 → V0
Path alternates: unmatched (U2-V1), matched (V1-U0), unmatched (U0-V0)
Augment by flipping:
  U2-V1: unmatched → matched
  U0-V1: matched → unmatched
  U0-V0: unmatched → matched
New matching: U0-V0, U2-V1
Matching size increased from 1 to 2

examples.input: Matching: U0-V0, U1-V1. Find augmenting path from U2
examples.trace:
Current matching edges: U0-V0, U1-V1
Unmatched vertices: U2, V2
Check possible paths from U2:
  U2 → V0 → U0 → V1 → U1 → V2: This path has consecutive matched edges (V0-U0 and V1-U1), not alternating
  U2 → V1 → U1 → V2: This has matched edge then unmatched, but starts with U2-V1 which doesn't exist
No valid alternating path from U2 to V2 exists
Therefore no augmentation possible

ladders.title: Building Block — Bipartite Graph Representation and Matching Arrays
ladders.desc: How to represent bipartite graphs and track matches between partitions
ladders.explanation: This level covers the data structures needed for bipartite matching algorithms. A bipartite graph is represented with two partitions U and V, typically as integer indices. We use an adjacency list where adj[u] contains all vertices v in V that u is connected to. The matching is tracked using two arrays: matchU[u] stores which vertex in V is matched to u (or -1 if unmatched), and matchV[v] stores which vertex in U is matched to v (or -1 if unmatched). This dual representation allows O(1) checks for whether a vertex is matched and to whom. The intuition is that we need efficient lookup and update operations for the matching state. During DFS, we use these arrays to check if a neighbor is matched, and if so, to find its partner for recursive search. The bipartite property ensures no edges exist within the same partition, which guarantees that augmenting paths have the alternating structure. This connects to the core logic (L2) as the implementation foundation for tracking matches during augmentation. Common mistakes include using a single matching array that doesn't allow O(1) lookups from both sides, not handling -1 values correctly for unmatched vertices, or incorrectly assuming the graph is bipartite without verification.

examples.input: U vertices: 0,1,2; V vertices: 3,4,5; edges: 0-3, 0-4, 1-3, 1-5, 2-4
examples.trace:
Create adjacency list for U partition:
  Vertex 0: connected to 3 and 4 → [3,4]
  Vertex 1: connected to 3 and 5 → [3,5]
  Vertex 2: connected to 4 → [4]
Initialize matching arrays:
  matchU has 3 slots for U vertices 0,1,2: all -1 (unmatched)
  matchV has 3 slots for V vertices 3,4,5: all -1 (unmatched)
Total vertices: |U| + |V| = 3 + 3 = 6
Edges: 5 edges in adjacency list

examples.input: After matching U0-V3 and U1-V5
examples.trace:
Update matchU array:
  U0 matched to V3 → matchU[0] = 3
  U1 matched to V5 → matchU[1] = 5
  U2 unmatched → matchU[2] = -1
Update matchV array (note V vertices are 3,4,5 indexed as 0,1,2 in array):
  V3 (index 0) matched to U0 → matchV[0] = 0
  V4 (index 1) unmatched → matchV[1] = -1
  V5 (index 2) matched to U1 → matchV[2] = 1
Check consistency: matchU[0]=3 and matchV[0]=0 agree

testCases.expected: U0-V2, U2-V3, U1 unmatched, V0 unmatched, V1-U0, V3-U2
U1-V3, U2-V1, U0 unmatched, V0-U2, V2 unmatched
U0-V0, U1-V2, U2 unmatched, V0-U0, V1 unmatched, V2-U1
U0-V1, U2-V0, U1 unmatched, V0-U2, V2 unmatched

ladders.title: Basic Operation — DFS Traversal with Backtracking
ladders.desc: Depth-first search that explores all paths and backtracks when stuck
ladders.explanation: This level covers the fundamental DFS traversal with backtracking, which is the engine of the matching algorithm. DFS explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking. In the context of bipartite matching, we use DFS to explore potential augmenting paths. The algorithm starts from a vertex, explores one neighbor completely (including recursive DFS from that neighbor), then backtracks to try other neighbors if the first doesn't lead to a solution. The key insight is that we need to mark vertices as visited during the current DFS call to avoid cycles, but we must clear these marks between different starting vertices. Backtracking happens when a recursive call returns False - we undo any temporary changes and try the next option. The intuition is that we're systematically exploring all possible paths from a starting vertex until we find one that works or exhaust all possibilities. Time complexity for one DFS from a vertex is O(V+E) in general. Space complexity is O(V) for recursion stack. This connects to the building block (L3) as the traversal mechanism that uses the graph representation. Common mistakes include not implementing proper backtracking (permanently changing state), not marking/unmarking visited vertices correctly, or having infinite recursion in graphs with cycles.

examples.input: Start at vertex 0, graph: 0->1->2->3, 0->4->5
examples.trace:
Start DFS at vertex 0
  Mark 0 as visited
  Explore neighbor 1
    Mark 1 as visited
    Explore neighbor 2
      Mark 2 as visited
      Explore neighbor 3
        Mark 3 as visited
        No more neighbors, return
      Backtrack from 3
    Backtrack from 2
  Backtrack from 1
  Explore neighbor 4
    Mark 4 as visited
    Explore neighbor 5
      Mark 5 as visited
      No more neighbors, return
    Backtrack from 5
  Backtrack from 4
Backtrack from 0
Visited order: 0,1,2,3,4,5

examples.input: Start at vertex A, graph: A->B, A->C, B->D, C->D
examples.trace:
Start DFS at A
  Mark A visited
  Explore neighbor B
    Mark B visited
    Explore neighbor D
      Mark D visited
      No more neighbors, return
    Backtrack from D
  Backtrack from B
  Explore neighbor C
    Mark C visited
    Explore neighbor D
      D already visited, skip
      No more neighbors, return
    Backtrack from C
  Backtrack from A
Paths found: A-B-D and A-C (D already visited)

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Graph Basics and DFS
ladders.desc: Fundamental graph concepts and depth-first search algorithm
ladders.explanation: This is the foundational level covering basic graph theory concepts needed for bipartite matching. A graph consists of vertices (nodes) and edges connecting them. Graphs can be represented as adjacency lists (list of neighbors for each vertex) or adjacency matrices. Depth-First Search (DFS) is a fundamental graph traversal algorithm that explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking. DFS uses a stack (either explicitly or via recursion) to keep track of vertices to visit. The algorithm starts at a source vertex, marks it as visited, then recursively visits all unvisited neighbors. This creates a depth-first exploration pattern. The key intuition is 'go deep first, then wide'. DFS has time complexity O(V+E) and space complexity O(V) for the visited set and recursion stack. For bipartite matching, we need to understand that a bipartite graph is one whose vertices can be divided into two disjoint sets such that every edge connects a vertex from one set to a vertex from the other set (no edges within the same set). This connects to the basic operation (L4) as the simplest form of graph traversal. Common beginner mistakes include not marking vertices as visited (causing infinite loops), not handling disconnected graphs, or confusing DFS with BFS (breadth-first search).

examples.input: Graph: vertices A,B,C,D; edges: A-B, A-C, B-D, C-D. Start DFS from A
examples.output: Visited order: A, B, D, C
examples.trace:
Start at A
  Mark A visited
  Explore neighbor B (first neighbor)
    Mark B visited
    Explore neighbor D (from B)
      Mark D visited
      D's neighbors: B (visited), C
      Explore C
        Mark C visited
        C's neighbors: A (visited), D (visited)
        Backtrack
      Backtrack
    Backtrack
  Explore neighbor C (from A)
    C already visited, skip
  DFS complete
Visitation order: A → B → D → C

examples.input: Graph: vertices 0,1,2,3; edges: 0-1, 0-2, 1-3. Start DFS from 0
examples.trace:
Start at 0
  Mark 0 visited
  Explore neighbor 1
    Mark 1 visited
    Explore neighbor 3
      Mark 3 visited
      3 has no neighbors
      Backtrack
    Backtrack
  Explore neighbor 2
    Mark 2 visited
    2 has no neighbors
    Backtrack
  Backtrack
Visitation order: 0 → 1 → 3 → 2

approaches.name: Hopcroft-Karp O(sqrt(V)*E)
approaches.summary: Use BFS to find multiple shortest augmenting paths simultaneously, then apply DFS to augment them in phases. This reduces the number of phases to sqrt(V), significantly speeding up matching in bipartite graphs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Implement Hopcroft-Karp Algorithm for Maximum Bipartite Matching
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of Hopcroft-Karp algorithm to find maximum matching in bipartite graphs.
ladders.explanation: The Hopcroft-Karp algorithm finds maximum bipartite matching in O(√V·E) time by processing multiple augmenting paths simultaneously in phases. The intuition is that by finding all shortest augmenting paths in each BFS phase and augmenting them via DFS, we reduce the number of phases from O(V) to O(√V). The algorithm maintains two partitions (U and V sets) and uses distance arrays to track shortest paths from unmatched U vertices. Each phase consists of: 1) BFS to build layered graph and find distances, 2) DFS to find vertex-disjoint augmenting paths along shortest distances, 3) Augmenting all found paths simultaneously. The key insight is that after each phase, the shortest augmenting path length increases, and there can be at most √V phases before no augmenting paths exist. Time complexity is O(√V·E) because each phase processes all edges via BFS/DFS, and there are O(√V) phases. Space is O(V+E) for adjacency lists and distance arrays. Connection to L1: This integrates the BFS layering and DFS augmentation subroutines into a complete algorithm. Common mistakes include: not resetting visited flags properly between DFS calls, incorrectly handling distance arrays when no augmenting paths exist, and forgetting to update both directions when augmenting edges. Real code pattern: Initialize matching arrays as -1, while BFS finds augmenting paths: run BFS to compute distances, run DFS from all free U vertices to find augmenting paths, augment all found paths.

examples.trace: Phase 1: BFS from free U vertices {0,1,2}. Distances: U[0]=0, U[1]=0, U[2]=0, V[3]=1, V[4]=1, V[5]=1. DFS from U0: path U0-V3, augment → match[0]=3. DFS from U1: path U1-V4, augment → match[1]=4. DFS from U2: path U2-V5, augment → match[2]=5. All vertices matched, matching size=3. Total phases=1.

Phase 1: BFS from free U vertices {0,1,2}. Distances: U[0]=0, U[1]=0, U[2]=0, V[3]=1, V[4]=1. DFS from U0: path U0-V3, augment → match[0]=3. DFS from U1: tries V3 but matched, backtrack, tries no other, fails. DFS from U2: path U2-V4, augment → match[2]=4. Matching size=2. Phase 2: BFS from free U vertex {1}. Distances: U[1]=0, V[3]=1 (via U0), V[4]=1 (via U2). DFS from U1: tries V3, path U1-V3-U0-V4-U2 (alternating), but V4 matched to U2, backtrack, no augmenting path found. Algorithm terminates with matching size=2.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — BFS Layering and DFS Augmentation for Multiple Paths
ladders.desc: Implement BFS to find all shortest augmenting paths and DFS to augment them simultaneously.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core phase of Hopcroft-Karp where we find and augment multiple shortest augmenting paths. The intuition is to efficiently discover all vertex-disjoint augmenting paths of the same shortest length in one go. BFS builds a layered graph starting from all unmatched U vertices, computing distances to reachable V vertices. The distance array helps DFS only follow edges that maintain the shortest path property. DFS then recursively explores from unmatched U vertices, following edges to V vertices with distance = current distance + 1, then to matched U vertices with distance = current distance + 2, etc., until finding an unmatched V vertex. When found, the path is augmented by flipping matched/unmatched status along the path. Multiple DFS calls can run in parallel because paths are vertex-disjoint. Time complexity per phase is O(E) for BFS+DFS. Space is O(V) for distance arrays. Connection to L0: This sub-routine is called repeatedly in the main loop until no augmenting paths exist. Connection to L2: This implements the simultaneous path processing technique. Common mistakes: DFS must mark vertices as visited during each call to avoid revisiting, but reset between calls; BFS must use INF distance for unreachable vertices; augmentation must update both endpoints' matching pointers. Real code pattern: BFS(queue, distU, distV) returns True if augmenting path exists; DFS(u, visited) returns True if augmenting path found from u.

examples.input: U vertices: 0(unmatched),1(unmatched), V vertices: 2(matched to 0),3(unmatched), edges: U0-V2, U0-V3, U1-V2, current matching: {(0,2)}
examples.output: Augmenting paths found: U0-V3, U1-V2-U0-V3 (after backtracking)
examples.trace: BFS: Start from unmatched U {0,1}. Distances: U0=0, U1=0. From U0: V2=1, V3=1. From U1: V2=1. Queue: V2(dist1), V3(dist1). Process V2: it's matched to U0, so U0 gets dist2. Process V3: unmatched, BFS stops. Distances: U0=0, U1=0, V2=1, V3=1, U0=2. DFS from U0: try V3 (dist1), V3 unmatched → augment path U0-V3, match U0=3. DFS from U1: try V2 (dist1), V2 matched to U0, try U0 (dist2), U0 now matched to V3, try V3 (dist3) but V3 unmatched? Wait, V3 now matched to U0, so path U1-V2-U0-V3 fails because V3 is matched. Actually, after first DFS, matching updated, so second DFS from U1: try V2 (dist1), V2 matched to U0, try U0 (dist2), U0 matched to V3, try V3 (dist3) but V3 is matched (to U0), dead end. So only one augmenting path found.

examples.input: U vertices: 0(unmatched),1(unmatched), V vertices: 2(unmatched),3(unmatched), edges: U0-V2, U0-V3, U1-V2, current matching: {}
examples.output: Augmenting paths found: U0-V2, U1-V3 (via U0-V3)
examples.trace: BFS: Start from unmatched U {0,1}. Distances: U0=0, U1=0. From U0: V2=1, V3=1. From U1: V2=1. Queue: V2(dist1), V3(dist1). Process V2: unmatched, BFS stops. Process V3: unmatched, BFS stops. Distances: U0=0, U1=0, V2=1, V3=1. DFS from U0: try V2 (dist1), V2 unmatched → augment path U0-V2, match U0=2. DFS from U1: try V2 (dist1) but V2 now matched to U0, backtrack, try no other from U1? Actually U1 only connected to V2, so fails. Wait, but we could have alternative: DFS from U0 could choose V3 instead. Let's redo: DFS from U0: try V3 (dist1), V3 unmatched → augment path U0-V3, match U0=3. Then DFS from U1: try V2 (dist1), V2 unmatched → augment path U1-V2, match U1=2. So two augmenting paths found if DFS chooses wisely. In practice, DFS order matters, but algorithm ensures vertex-disjoint paths by marking visited.

testCases.expected: Augmenting paths: 0-2 and 1-? (depends on DFS order)

testCases.input: U={0(unmatched),1(matched to 2)}, V={2(matched to 1),3(unmatched)}, edges=[(0,2),(0,3),(1,2)], matching={(1,2)}
U={0(matched to 1),1(unmatched)}, V={1(matched to 0),2(unmatched)}, edges=[(0,1),(1,2)], matching={(0,1)}

ladders.title: Core Logic — Simultaneous Processing of Shortest Augmenting Paths
ladders.desc: Understand why processing multiple shortest augmenting paths per phase reduces phases to O(√V).
ladders.explanation: The core insight of Hopcroft-Karp is that by finding all shortest augmenting paths in a phase and augmenting them simultaneously, the number of phases is bounded by O(√V). Intuition: In bipartite matching, augmenting along a path increases matching size by 1. If we only augment one shortest path at a time, there could be O(V) phases. But if we augment all shortest paths of length L in one phase, the next shortest augmenting path must have length at least L+2. This is because augmenting along vertex-disjoint paths doesn't create shorter new paths. Over phases, the shortest path length increases: after k phases, the shortest augmenting path length is at least 2k+1. Since the maximum possible path length is V, we have 2k+1 ≤ V, so k ≤ (V-1)/2, but this is still O(V). The key improvement comes from a combinatorial lemma: after each phase, the number of remaining unmatched vertices decreases significantly. Specifically, if the current matching size is m, and we augment along all shortest paths of length L, then the number of remaining phases is O(√V). More formally, after √V phases, either matching is maximum or shortest path length > √V, and there can be at most √V such phases. Time per phase is O(E), so total O(√V·E). Connection to L1: This logic justifies why the BFS/DFS sub-routine is repeated √V times. Connection to L3: This builds on the concept of augmenting paths. Common mistakes: Misunderstanding why paths must be vertex-disjoint for simultaneous augmentation; thinking the bound is O(log V) phases. Real code pattern: While BFS finds augmenting paths: run phase, increment matching size by number of paths found.

examples.input: Graph with V=4 vertices, initial matching size=0, after first phase augment 2 paths of length 1
examples.trace: Phase 1: Find all shortest augmenting paths of length 1 (direct edges from free U to free V). Augment 2 such paths simultaneously. Now matching size=2. Consider any new augmenting path: it must start from a free U, go to a matched V, then to its matched U, then to a free V. That's length at least 3. So next phase will find paths of length 3 or more.

examples.input: Graph with V=9, initial matching size=0, phases with shortest path lengths: 1,3,5
examples.trace: Phase 1: Augment all length-1 paths. Suppose 3 paths augmented. Matching size=3. Phase 2: Shortest augmenting paths now length 3. Augment some paths. Matching size increases. Phase 3: Shortest paths length 5. Augment. After at most 3 phases, either matching is maximum (size maybe 4 or 5) or no augmenting paths left. In worst case, √V=3 phases suffice.

testCases.input: V=4, initial matching=0, augment 1 path of length 1 in phase 1

ladders.title: Building Block — Augmenting Paths in Bipartite Matching
ladders.desc: Find and augment a single augmenting path to increase matching size.
ladders.explanation: An augmenting path is a path that starts and ends at unmatched vertices, alternating between unmatched and matched edges. In bipartite graphs, augmenting paths have odd length and alternate between U and V partitions. The intuition is that flipping the status of edges along an augmenting path (unmatched→matched and matched→unmatched) increases the matching size by 1. This is because the path has one more unmatched edge than matched edges at the start. To find an augmenting path, we can use BFS or DFS from all unmatched U vertices, following these rules: from a U vertex, go to any adjacent V vertex; from a V vertex, if matched, go to its matched U vertex, else if unmatched, we've found the end of the path. This builds an alternating tree. Time complexity to find one path is O(E) via BFS. Space is O(V) for visited markers and parent pointers. Connection to L2: Augmenting paths are what we process simultaneously in Hopcroft-Karp. Connection to L4: This uses graph traversal as a basic operation. Common mistakes: Not ensuring the path alternates correctly; forgetting to update both endpoints when augmenting; infinite loops if revisiting vertices. Real code pattern: BFS(queue, parentU, parentV) to find path from free U to free V, then while v != -1: u = parentV[v], match u and v, v = parentU[u].

examples.input: U vertices: 0(unmatched),1(matched to 2), V vertices: 2(matched to 1),3(unmatched), edges: U0-V2, U0-V3, U1-V2
examples.trace: Start BFS from unmatched U0. Level 0: U0. From U0: go to V2 (matched) and V3 (unmatched). If go to V3: it's unmatched, so path found: U0-V3. Augment: match U0 and V3. If go to V2 first: V2 is matched to U1, so enqueue U1. From U1: go to V2 (but already visited), no other edges. Could also find path U0-V2-U1-? but U1 has no other connections, so dead end. So simplest path is U0-V3.

examples.input: U vertices: 0(unmatched),1(unmatched), V vertices: 2(unmatched),3(unmatched), edges: U0-V2, U0-V3, U1-V2
examples.output: Augmenting path: U0-V2 or U0-V3 or U1-V2
examples.trace: BFS from U0: go to V2 (unmatched), path U0-V2 found. Augment: match U0 and V2. Alternatively, from U0 go to V3: path U0-V3. Or BFS from U1: go to V2, path U1-V2. Any of these increases matching from 0 to 1.

testCases.input: U={0(unmatched),1(matched to 2)}, V={2(matched to 1),3(matched to 4)}, edges=[(0,2),(0,3)], U2 not exist, assume U={0,1} only

ladders.title: Basic Operation — BFS/DFS Traversal on Bipartite Graphs
ladders.desc: Perform breadth-first or depth-first search on a bipartite graph to explore vertices and edges.
ladders.explanation: Graph traversal is fundamental to navigating bipartite graphs. BFS explores level by level, using a queue, which is useful for finding shortest paths. DFS explores as deep as possible using recursion or a stack, useful for exhaustive search. In bipartite graphs, vertices are partitioned into sets U and V, and edges only connect U to V (no within-set edges). Traversal must respect this bipartite structure: from a U vertex, we only go to V vertices; from a V vertex, only to U vertices. This alternation is natural in BFS/DFS if we track the partition. Time complexity is O(V+E) for full traversal. Space is O(V) for visited array and queue/stack. Connection to L3: Traversal is used to find augmenting paths. Connection to L5: This builds on basic graph concepts. Common mistakes: Forgetting to mark vertices as visited leading to cycles; incorrect adjacency list representation; not handling disconnected graphs. Real code pattern: BFS: queue = [start], visited[start]=True, while queue: u = queue.pop(0), for v in adj[u]: if not visited[v]: visited[v]=True, queue.append(v).

examples.input: U={0,1}, V={2,3}, edges: U0-V2, U0-V3, U1-V2, start BFS from U0
examples.output: BFS order: U0, V2, V3, U1
examples.trace: Start at U0 (level 0). Visit neighbors: V2 and V3 (level 1). From V2: its neighbor is U1 (since only edge U1-V2) (level 2). From V3: no neighbors beyond U0. From U1: neighbor V2 already visited. So order: U0, V2, V3, U1.

examples.input: Same graph, start DFS from U0
examples.output: DFS order depends on implementation, e.g., U0, V2, U1, V3
examples.trace: Start at U0. Go to V2 (first neighbor). From V2, go to U1. From U1, go to V2 but visited, backtrack. Then from U0, go to V3. So order: U0, V2, U1, V3.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Bipartite Graphs and Matching Basics
ladders.desc: Understand what bipartite graphs are and what a matching means.
ladders.explanation: A bipartite graph is a graph whose vertices can be divided into two disjoint sets U and V such that every edge connects a vertex in U to a vertex in V. There are no edges within U or within V. This models relationships between two types of objects, like users and resources, or tasks and workers. A matching is a set of edges without common vertices, meaning no two edges share an endpoint. In bipartite matching, we typically match vertices from U to V. The goal is to find a maximum matching, which is a matching with the largest possible number of edges. A vertex is matched if it is incident to an edge in the matching; otherwise, it is free or unmatched. Basic concepts: perfect matching matches all vertices, maximum matching is largest possible. Intuition: Matching represents pairing where each vertex is paired with at most one partner. Connection to L4: Matching problems are solved using graph traversal on bipartite graphs. Common mistakes: Confusing bipartite with general graphs; thinking matching can have edges sharing vertices. Real code pattern: Represent graph as adjacency lists: adjU for U vertices listing neighbors in V, adjV for V vertices listing neighbors in U.

examples.input: U={students: A,B}, V={projects: X,Y}, edges: A-X, A-Y, B-X
examples.output: Possible matchings: {A-X} size 1, {A-Y} size 1, {B-X} size 1, {A-X, B-X} invalid (shares X), maximum matching size=1
examples.trace: Graph is bipartite: students in U, projects in V. Edges only between U and V. Matching: can match A to X (edge A-X), or A to Y, or B to X. Cannot match both A and B to X because X would be shared. So maximum matching has 1 edge, e.g., {A-X}.
Bipartite graph: U={0,1}, V={2,3}. All possible edges between sets. We can match 0 to 2 and 1 to 3, giving 2 edges with no shared vertices. This is maximum because all vertices can be matched.
